diagrams.title: Basic DVR Network Topology
diagrams.description: Simple network with 4 routers (A, B, C, D) connected in a topology showing how distance vectors are exchanged
elements: Router A connected to B and C
Router B connected to A and D
Router C connected to A and D
Link costs: A-B=2, A-C=4, B-D=3, C-D=5
diagrams.purpose: Understanding basic DVR operation and neighbor relationships

diagrams.title: Routing Table Structure
diagrams.description: Shows the format of a typical routing table in DVR with destination, next-hop, and metric columns
diagrams.purpose: Understanding what information routers maintain and exchange

diagrams.title: Count-to-Infinity Problem
diagrams.description: Illustration showing how routing loop occurs between two routers when a network becomes unreachable
elements: Link X-Y breaks, Y thinks X has path to N
X and Y increment metrics indefinitely: 1,2,3...15,16
diagrams.purpose: Visualizing the routing loop problem and need for solutions

diagrams.title: Split Horizon Example
diagrams.description: Shows how split horizon prevents advertising routes back to source
elements: Router A learns route to D via B
Router A does NOT advertise route to D back to B
Arrow showing A→B for other routes but no return arrow for D

diagrams.title: Bellman-Ford Calculation
diagrams.description: Step-by-step calculation showing how minimum path cost is determined using the Bellman-Ford equation
elements: Source router S, intermediate I, destination D
Example: S-A=2, A-D=3, S-B=4, B-D=1 → S-B-D=5 is best
